title: Podman kube play and environement variables
body:
### Feature request description

Podman Kube Play needs some way of settings environment variables from an external sources. --env=KEY=value would be simple example of a variable that we which to pass along, but we also have more complex examples like:

**.env**
```env
POSTGRES_USER=${USERNAME}
POSTGRES_PASSWORD=Developer2025!
```

## The competition

The biggest reason to have this, is because Docker has it. docker-compose is easier to work with for developers since they support an external .env file or a single variable. The competition even goes one step further and automatically takes any .env in the folder it starts, and parses it.

## The challenge

As a developer, I strife to be closely connected to the production environments. That said, I must also find easy ways of getting our work done and right now, docker-compose has just less friction from day-to-day.

This is not the first time that this is discussed, https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/21057, but it would be nice to keep the discussion going since it's in the benefit of podman to offer easy migration paths for developers.

# Suggest potential solution

```bash
podman kube play kube.yaml --env=POSTGRES_USER=${USERNAME}
```

```bash
podman kube play kube.yaml --env-file=.env
```

```bash
podman kube play kube.yaml --env-host
```

All three of these examples are based on the CLI for Podman iteslf. After all, it's already possible to pass environment variables to a single container. By extending the CLI in this way, it's consistent and user-friendly.

I do object to docker's auto-parsing of .env files who happen to be in proximity. Podman could strife for a best-of-both worlds solution by extending the functionality of existing parameters, without implementing 'new' logic.

## Future plans?

in the future, we could discuss the specification itself and see if we can extend it:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: postgres
      image: docker.io/library/postgres:17
      env:
        - name: POSTGRES_USER
          valueFromShell: ${USERNAME}
        - file: .env          
        - host: true
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: my-secret
envData:
  path: .env  
```

This is out of scope for now though, since changing a specification like this takes years.

# Have you considered any alternatives?

There are only workarounds. I could use a template file and use `sed` or `envsubst` but that is less elegant and it makes settings up a development environment just that little bit harder.

# Additional context

I'm reviewing Podman and its tooling within a company context so while I might have less trouble embracing a workaround, I also have to sell this tooling to the rest of the team: Thus, more friction than docker is a bad thing.

comments.body: @antverpp I'm breathing new life into this issue

@ygalblum You previously objected to a [related change](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/21098) and I think that I agree with you. It would be unwise to parse files from a .yaml if the specification does not support that.

Still, I hope that what I propose for the CLI, is something that you see a benefit in.

Would the cli env then pass the variables into all containers in the yaml? I guess logic wise this wouldn't really make sense as most envs are container specific.

As for the file syntax in general we should just support what k8s supports so I do agree that we can only do this via some cli options.

> --env=${USERNAME}

That is not how the podman run --env works. The syntax is either --env=KEY (which reads the value from the process environment) or --env=KEY=value.
Similar for an env file. Making play kube options differ from play kube and require different parsing would be a bad thing IMO.

@Luap99 Good catch with the --env example, I corrected it in the main post.

> Would the cli env then pass the variables into all containers in the yaml? I guess logic wise this wouldn't really make sense as most envs are container specific.

I think that is a fair compromise. If a developer or administrator wants more control, he has to create the containers individually with the correct .env, and assign them to the shared pod. 

This also highlights one of the reasons why it has not been implemented yet: It's not very elegant. I think it's worth the trade-off since podman already supports these variables on a container level. In fact... I would argue that --env-host is also a bit messy, but a worthwhile compromise.

> The biggest reason to have this, is because Docker has it. docker-compose is easier to work with for developers since they support an external .env file or a single variable. The competition even goes one step further and automatically takes any .env in the folder it starts, and parses it.

I find that comparing Compose with k8s manifests is a bit unfair in terms of developer experience. One is designed to coordinate containers, while the other also manages many other resources and behaviors. Not to mention, you don't even have the concept of Pod when working with Compose itself. I don't want to be pedantic here, but my point is that though they seem the same, Compose is a subset of a k8s manifest, and they don't need to be treated the same way.

I understand you mentioned `envsubst` and `sed` as alternatives, but have you also considered [Kustomize](https://kustomize.io/)? 

I feel like the best way to work with Kubernetes manifests is to leverage the tooling present in this ecosystem, and for that specific case we have a very mature one that is Kustomize, which can definitely help you with this scenario and do so much more. [Here's an example](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/24345) of how you can load your envs onto a ConfigMap. You can do the same and load them to a Secret by using the [secret generator](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configmap-secret/managing-secret-using-kustomize/) as well. You get a greater benefit of properly splitting your yamls on whatever the logic you seem to enjoy the most, and all you need to do is to `kubectl kustomize | podman kube play -`.

Moreover, I would advocate against rolling our own custom CRDs for Podman (or worse, changing the behavior of the existing ones) as that will decrease compatibility between the specs.

Now, as for having a built-in support. I find this can be tricky, as you need to take into consideration how the template processing will replace the variables. In a k8s manifest, there's no such thing as env variable interpolation throughout the file, but only on a small portion, which is the `env` section of a PodSpec. And their syntax is slightly different than Compose, [as documented here](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/inject-data-application/define-interdependent-environment-variables/#define-an-environment-dependent-variable-for-a-container). Also, you need to take into account how the variable definition can be interpolated themselves, and you have to evaluate them at definition time to get the effect of `MYVARIABLE="$MYOTHERVAR"` to work as they do in bash. Then, you also have to deal with the many sources that an env variable can be populated from in a PodSpec: `env.valueFrom`, `envFrom` and manage their order of preference.

> I understand you mentioned envsubst and sed as alternatives, but have you also considered [Kustomize](https://kustomize.io/)?

I understand that there are workarounds, but I'm more interested in an integrated version. If the Podman Kube CLI had some integrated way of supporting environment variables, then that would create a bridge between Docker Compose.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

> A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Noted. But, simply marking this issue as `stale`, only puts it under the rug.

@Eonfge I feel you...:

```
podman kube play kompose.yaml 
Error: encountered while bringing up pod clamav-pod: parsing reference "${PODMAN_CLAMAV_IMAGE}": invalid reference format
```

So how to you work around this flaw? Do you even use .env files anymore with `podman kube play`? Any suggestions would be helpful 😀

This doesn't really address the ask, but one work around is to use a ConfigMap instead of something like a .env for `podman kube play`.

```yaml
# configmap.yaml
#
# If this has sensitive data in it, be sure to gitignore it.
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: thingy
data:
  FOO: bar
```

And then:

```sh
podman kube play ./something.yaml --configmap ./configmap.yaml
```

I like this approach because it's consistent with the rest of the whole Kubernetes spec in Podman thing, and you can inject environment variables into your app with no ambiguity or magic.

I don't like this approach because you may need a .env file anyway and it's lame to have to duplicate this data. I almost wish `podman kube play` could just have some sort of `$$` syntax so you could get envsubst behavior for free.

Edit: I see now @kriansa already pointed this out. Leaving up my thing because it's a little more straight-forward an explanation.
